summary: DHH shares a humorous list of phrases that managers use to shut down technical arguments they're losing. The post highlights common deflection tactics like appealing to 'the real world,' deferring to future versions, or claiming past failure without evidence. It's a very early, brief blog post that foreshadows DHH's career-long skepticism of management authority over technical decisions.
key_points: Managers often use stock phrases to end technical debates they're losing rather than engage with the substance
Common deflection tactics include appeals to experience ('we tried that'), complexity ('N-tier architecture'), or deferral ('version 2.0')
The underlying dynamic is that managers would rather look foolish than lose a technical argument publicly
money_quotes: Top five things to say when you're losing a technical argument (and want to look like a fool)
Yes, well, that's just not the way things work in the real world.
It would probably be best if we deferred that until version 2.0.
The world would come to an end if they lost, of course.
key_insight: Managers who can't win technical arguments on merit resort to a predictable set of dismissive phrases that prioritize ego over good engineering decisions.
